title: Why Clean Code is a Lie We Tell Seniors.
excerpt: Exploring the pragmatism of 'good enough' code over academic perfection in high-growth startups.

blocks.heading: The Perfection Trap
blocks.body: Clean code is often sold as an objective truth, a set of rules that, if followed, will lead to software immortality. But in the trenches of a high-growth startup, 'clean' is a moving target. What looks like an elegant abstraction today is often a rigid cage tomorrow.

blocks.text: Code is a liability, not an asset. The less of it you have to maintain, the better. Academic purity is for textbooks; production is for value.

blocks.heading: The Cost of Over-Engineering
blocks.body: Many seniors fall into the trap of over-abstracting before they even understand the domain. They build complex generic factories for logic that will only ever run in one place. This isn't just wasted time—it's a maintenance nightmare.

blocks.heading: Don't Repeat Yourself (DRY) vs WET
blocks.body: We've been taught that duplication is the root of all evil. But premature abstraction is far more dangerous. Duplication is cheap; the wrong abstraction is expensive. Sometimes, it's better to Write Everything Twice (WET) before you find the true pattern.

blocks.heading: The 3 Pillars of Pragmatic Code
items: Delete-ability: Can I remove this code in 5 minutes without the system collapsing?
Readability: Can a junior developer understand this on their first day?
Value: Does this line of code actually move the needle for the product?

blocks.caption: The reality of production: Structure is essential, but flexibility is survival.

blocks.heading: Conclusion
blocks.body: Stop chasing the ghost of perfect code. Aim for boring, predictable, and simple. Your job isn't to be an artist; it's to be an architect who builds things that don't fall down when the requirements inevitably change next week.
